kids.question: Who told Joshua to be strong and of a good courage?
kids.explanation: Joshua 1:9 — "Have not I commanded thee? Be strong and of a good courage; be not afraid, neither be thou dismayed: for the LORD thy God is with thee whithersoever thou goest."

kids.question: What land was God giving to the Israelites?
kids.explanation: Joshua 1:2 — "Moses my servant is dead; now therefore arise, go over this Jordan, thou, and all this people, unto the land which I do give to them."

kids.question: What river did God tell Joshua and the people to cross?
kids.explanation: Joshua 1:2 — "Arise, go over this Jordan, thou, and all this people, unto the land which I do give to them."

teens.question: What did God tell Joshua to meditate on day and night?
teens.option_a: This book of the law
teens.explanation: Joshua 1:8 — "This book of the law shall not depart out of thy mouth; but thou shalt meditate therein day and night."

teens.question: What promise did God give about Joshua's territory?
teens.option_a: Every place the sole of his foot trod upon He would give him
teens.option_b: Every city he attacked would fall
teens.option_c: He would defeat all enemies in one year
teens.option_d: His borders would match those of Moses
teens.explanation: Joshua 1:3 — "Every place that the sole of your foot shall tread upon, that have I given unto you, as I said unto Moses."

teens.question: Which two tribes and a half tribe had already received their inheritance on the east side of Jordan?
teens.option_a: Reuben, Gad, and half of Manasseh
teens.option_b: Judah, Benjamin, and half of Ephraim
teens.option_c: Simeon, Levi, and half of Dan
teens.option_d: Issachar, Zebulun, and half of Naphtali
teens.explanation: Joshua 1:12-13 — "And to the Reubenites, and to the Gadites, and to half the tribe of Manasseh, spake Joshua, saying, Remember the word which Moses the servant of the LORD commanded you."

teens.question: What did the people promise they would do to anyone who rebelled against Joshua's commands?
teens.option_a: He would be put to death
teens.option_b: He would be cast out of the camp
teens.option_d: He would serve as a slave
teens.explanation: Joshua 1:18 — "Whosoever he be that doth rebel against thy commandment, and will not hearken unto thy words in all that thou commandest him, he shall be put to death."

teens.question: What condition did God attach to Joshua's prosperity and success?
teens.option_a: That he observe to do all the law and not turn from it
teens.option_c: That he appoint wise judges over the people
teens.option_d: That he build an altar at the Jordan
teens.explanation: Joshua 1:7-8 — "Only be thou strong and very courageous, that thou mayest observe to do according to all the law...that thou mayest prosper whithersoever thou goest."

adults.question: What were the exact northern and southern geographical boundaries God promised to Joshua?
adults.option_a: From the wilderness and Lebanon to the great river Euphrates and the great sea
adults.option_b: From the Sea of Galilee to the Negev
adults.option_c: From the Jordan to the Mediterranean coast
adults.option_d: From Sinai to Damascus
adults.explanation: Joshua 1:4 — "From the wilderness and this Lebanon even unto the great river, the river Euphrates...and unto the great sea toward the going down of the sun, shall be your coast."

adults.question: What specific promise concerning enemies did God give Joshua in verse 5?
adults.option_a: There shall not any man be able to stand before thee all the days of thy life
adults.option_b: No enemy would enter the camp
adults.option_c: He would defeat every enemy in battle
adults.option_d: His army would never suffer losses
adults.explanation: Joshua 1:5 — "There shall not any man be able to stand before thee all the days of thy life: as I was with Moses, so I will be with thee: I will not fail thee, nor forsake thee."

adults.question: How many times does God command Joshua to "be strong and courageous" in chapter 1?
adults.option_a: Three times
adults.option_b: Twice
adults.option_c: Once
adults.option_d: Four times
adults.explanation: Joshua 1:6, 1:7, and 1:9 each contain a form of the command "be strong and of a good courage," appearing three times in the chapter.

adults.question: What theological condition for success did God give in Joshua 1:8?
adults.option_a: Meditate on the law day and night and observe to do all that is written therein
adults.option_b: Build a tabernacle before entering the land
adults.option_c: Consult the Urim and Thummim before each battle
adults.option_d: Offer burnt offerings on the first day of each month
adults.explanation: Joshua 1:8 — "This book of the law shall not depart out of thy mouth; but thou shalt meditate therein day and night, that thou mayest observe to do according to all that is written therein: for then thou shalt make thy way prosperous."

adults.question: What was required of the two and a half tribes who had already received their portion east of Jordan?
adults.option_a: Their armed men were to pass before their brethren and help them take the land
adults.option_b: They were excused from further warfare
adults.option_c: They were to remain and guard their cities only
adults.option_d: They were to send half their warriors
adults.explanation: Joshua 1:14 — "Your wives, your little ones, and your cattle, shall remain in the land which Moses gave you...but ye shall pass before your brethren armed."

adults.question: What was the people's formal response to Joshua's leadership, and what precedent did they cite?
adults.option_a: All that thou commandest us we will do, as we hearkened unto Moses
adults.option_b: They pledged their swords to him
adults.option_c: They offered a sacrifice in his honour
adults.option_d: They proclaimed him king over Israel
adults.explanation: Joshua 1:16-17 — "All that thou commandest us we will do, and whithersoever thou sendest us, we will go. According as we hearkened unto Moses in all things, so will we hearken unto thee."

adults.question: What is the theological significance of God saying "I will not fail thee, nor forsake thee" to Joshua?
adults.option_a: It was a military guarantee only
adults.option_b: It is a covenantal assurance echoed in Hebrews 13:5, showing God's unchanging faithfulness to his servants
adults.option_c: It was a one-time promise limited to the conquest
adults.option_d: It showed Joshua was sinless
adults.explanation: Joshua 1:5 and Hebrews 13:5 — "I will never leave thee, nor forsake thee" — demonstrate that God's commitment to Joshua reflects his eternal covenant faithfulness to all who trust him.

adults.question: Why were the two and a half tribes' fighting men obligated to cross the Jordan with their brethren even though their own land was already secured?
adults.option_a: Moses had forced them
adults.option_b: Their agreement with Moses required them to assist their brethren until the whole land was given rest
adults.option_c: They had no cities to defend yet
adults.option_d: God commanded them at Sinai
adults.explanation: Joshua 1:13-15 — "Remember the word which Moses the servant of the LORD commanded you...ye shall return unto the land of your possession, and enjoy it, which Moses the LORD's servant gave you on this side Jordan."

kids.question: What woman hid the two spies that Joshua sent to Jericho?
kids.explanation: Joshua 2:1 — "Joshua...sent out of Shittim two men to spy secretly...and they came into an harlot's house, named Rahab, and lodged there."

kids.question: Where did Rahab hide the two spies?
kids.option_a: Under the stalks of flax on the roof
kids.option_c: In a cave outside the city
kids.option_d: In a cellar beneath her house
kids.explanation: Joshua 2:6 — "But she had brought them up to the roof of the house, and hid them with the stalks of flax, which she had laid in order upon the roof."

kids.question: What sign did Rahab hang in her window so the Israelites would spare her family?
kids.explanation: Joshua 2:21 — "And she bound the scarlet line in the window."

teens.question: What had the people of Jericho heard that caused their hearts to melt in fear?
teens.option_a: That Joshua was a mighty warrior
teens.option_b: That the LORD dried up the Red Sea and what Israel did to the Amorite kings
teens.option_c: That Israel had a great army
teens.option_d: That God spoke to Joshua from a cloud
teens.explanation: Joshua 2:10-11 — "For we have heard how the LORD dried up the water of the Red sea...and as soon as we had heard these things, our hearts did melt."

teens.question: What did Rahab confess about the LORD?
teens.option_a: That the LORD your God, he is God in heaven above and in earth beneath
teens.option_b: That he was a God of war
teens.option_c: That he was stronger than Baal
teens.option_d: That he would forgive all nations
teens.explanation: Joshua 2:11 — "For the LORD your God, he is God in heaven above, and in earth beneath."

teens.question: What condition did the spies place on their oath to Rahab?
teens.option_a: She had to bind the scarlet thread in the window and gather all her family into her house
teens.option_b: She had to leave the city before they came
teens.option_c: She had to tell no one
teens.option_d: She had to provide food for the army
teens.explanation: Joshua 2:18-19 — "Thou shalt bind this line of scarlet thread in the window...and thou shalt bring thy father, and thy mother, and thy brethren, and all thy father's household, home unto thee."

teens.question: What route did Rahab use to let the spies down from her house?
teens.option_a: She let them down by a cord through the window, for her house was on the city wall
teens.option_b: She bribed the guards to open the gate
teens.option_d: She led them through a tunnel
teens.explanation: Joshua 2:15 — "Then she let them down by a cord through the window: for her house was upon the town wall, and she dwelt upon the wall."

teens.question: Where did the spies hide for three days at Rahab's instruction before returning to Joshua?
teens.option_a: In the mountain
teens.option_b: In the house of a friendly merchant
teens.option_c: In a cave near the river
teens.option_d: In the fields outside the city
teens.explanation: Joshua 2:16 — "Get you to the mountain, lest the pursuers meet you; and hide yourselves there three days, until the pursuers be returned."

adults.question: What theological significance does Rahab's faith carry in the New Testament?
adults.option_a: She is cited in Hebrews 11:31 and James 2:25 as an example of saving faith demonstrated by works
adults.option_b: She is mentioned only in genealogies
adults.option_c: She represents the conversion of all Canaanites
adults.option_d: She is a type of the church
adults.explanation: Hebrews 11:31 — "By faith the harlot Rahab perished not with them that believed not." James 2:25 — "Was not Rahab the harlot justified by works, when she had received the messengers?"

adults.question: What specific intelligence did the spies report to Joshua after returning from Jericho?
adults.option_a: Truly the LORD hath delivered into our hands all the land; for even all the inhabitants of the country do faint because of us
adults.option_b: The city's walls were weakest at the north
adults.option_c: The city had a large army but few supplies
adults.option_d: The gates were guarded by night only
adults.explanation: Joshua 2:24 — "And they said unto Joshua, Truly the LORD hath delivered into our hands all the land; for even all the inhabitants of the country do faint because of us."

adults.question: What was Rahab's request in exchange for protecting the spies?
adults.option_a: That the Israelites would show kindness to her father's house and give a true token
adults.option_b: A portion of land in Canaan
adults.option_c: That she be made a princess in Israel
adults.option_d: That her debts be forgiven
adults.explanation: Joshua 2:12 — "Now therefore, I pray you, swear unto me by the LORD, since I have shewed you kindness, that ye will also shew kindness unto my father's house, and give me a true token."

adults.question: What was the kings' response when told the spies had come to Jericho?
adults.option_a: He sent men to Rahab's house demanding she bring out the men
adults.option_b: He ordered the city gates sealed
adults.option_c: He sent soldiers to the Jordan to intercept them
adults.option_d: He consulted his priests
adults.explanation: Joshua 2:3 — "And the king of Jericho sent unto Rahab, saying, Bring forth the men that are come to thee, which are entered into thine house: for they be come to search out all the country."

adults.question: What lie did Rahab tell the king's messengers, and what does this raise theologically?
adults.option_a: She said they had never arrived
adults.option_b: She said the men had gone out before the gate shut at dark and she did not know where they went
adults.option_c: She said they were merchants
adults.option_d: She said they had already left for Egypt
adults.explanation: Joshua 2:4-5 — "She said, There came men unto me, but I wist not whence they were...the men went out: whither the men went I wot not." This raises theological debate about deception in service of a greater good.

adults.question: What does the scarlet cord hung by Rahab typologically foreshadow in Christian interpretation?
adults.option_a: The law given at Sinai
adults.option_b: The blood of Christ that marks believers for salvation, parallel to the Passover blood on the doorposts
adults.option_c: The priestly garments of Aaron
adults.option_d: The red heifer offering
adults.explanation: The scarlet thread in Rahab's window (Joshua 2:21) has been interpreted by church fathers as a type of Christ's blood, analogous to the blood on the doorposts at Passover (Exodus 12:13).

adults.question: How does Rahab appear in the genealogy of Jesus in the New Testament?
adults.option_a: She is not mentioned
adults.option_b: She is listed in Matthew 1:5 as the mother of Boaz, placing her in the Messianic line
adults.option_c: She is mentioned in Luke's genealogy only
adults.option_d: She is listed as an ancestor of John the Baptist
adults.explanation: Matthew 1:5 — "And Salmon begat Booz of Rachab; and Booz begat Obed of Ruth; and Obed begat Jesse." Rahab is in the direct line leading to David and Christ.

adults.question: What phrase did Rahab use to describe the psychological state of Jericho's inhabitants, and what does this fulfil?
adults.option_a: They were angry and preparing for war
adults.option_b: All the inhabitants of the country do faint because of us — fulfilling the fear God had promised to send before Israel
adults.option_c: They were confident in their walls
adults.option_d: They were unaware of Israel's approach
adults.explanation: Joshua 2:24 and Exodus 23:27 — "I will send my fear before thee, and will destroy all the people to whom thou shalt come." Rahab's report confirms this divine promise was already being fulfilled.

kids.question: What did the priests carry into the Jordan River?
kids.explanation: Joshua 3:15 — "And as they that bare the ark were come unto Jordan, and the feet of the priests that bare the ark were dipped in the brim of the water."

kids.question: What happened to the Jordan River when the priests stepped into it?
kids.option_b: The waters stopped flowing and the people crossed on dry ground
kids.explanation: Joshua 3:16-17 — "The waters...rose up upon an heap...and the people passed over right against Jericho."

kids.question: How far behind the ark were the people told to stay?
kids.explanation: Joshua 3:4 — "Yet there shall be a space between you and it, about two thousand cubits by measure: come not near unto it."

teens.question: Why did Joshua tell the people to sanctify themselves before crossing the Jordan?
teens.option_a: Because they were entering enemy territory
teens.option_b: Because tomorrow the LORD would do wonders among them
teens.option_c: Because they needed to be pure to fight
teens.explanation: Joshua 3:5 — "And Joshua said unto the people, Sanctify yourselves: for to morrow the LORD will do wonders among you."

teens.question: What purpose did God give for parting the Jordan before all Israel?
teens.option_a: That all people might know the hand of the LORD is mighty and that they might fear him
teens.option_b: To prove Joshua was a great general
teens.option_c: To show the Canaanites Israel's power
teens.option_d: To fulfil a promise made to Abraham
teens.explanation: Joshua 4:24 — "That all the people of the earth might know the hand of the LORD, that it is mighty: that ye might fear the LORD your God for ever."

teens.question: Who bore the ark of the covenant during the Jordan crossing?
teens.option_a: The Levites
teens.option_b: The priests the sons of Levi
teens.explanation: Joshua 3:3 — "When ye see the ark of the covenant of the LORD your God, and the priests the Levites bearing it, then ye shall remove from your place."

teens.question: Where did the waters of Jordan pile up when they were cut off?
teens.option_a: Very far from Adam, the city beside Zaretan
teens.explanation: Joshua 3:16 — "The waters which came down from above stood and rose up upon an heap very far from the city Adam, that is beside Zaretan."

teens.question: At what season was Israel crossing the Jordan, and why was this significant?
teens.option_a: At harvest time when it overflowed all its banks, making the miracle greater
teens.option_b: In summer when it was low
teens.option_c: In winter when it was frozen
teens.option_d: In spring before the rains came
teens.explanation: Joshua 3:15 — "And Jordan overfloweth all his banks all the time of harvest." The flood-stage crossing magnified the miracle.

adults.question: What title is given to the ark in Joshua 3:11, and what theological significance does it carry?
adults.option_a: The ark of the covenant of the Lord of all the earth, declaring God's universal sovereignty
adults.option_b: The ark of God's glory
adults.option_c: The ark of the testimony
adults.option_d: The ark of the God of Israel
adults.explanation: Joshua 3:11 — "Behold, the ark of the covenant of the LORD of all the earth passeth over before you into Jordan." The title "LORD of all the earth" asserts divine sovereignty over Canaan and its gods.

adults.question: What parallel does the Jordan crossing draw with the Exodus event?
adults.option_a: Both involved manna falling from heaven
adults.option_b: Both involved God miraculously dividing waters to deliver Israel, establishing Joshua as a new Moses
adults.option_c: Both required the death of an enemy king
adults.option_d: Both occurred on the Passover
adults.explanation: Joshua 3:7 — "This day will I begin to magnify thee in the sight of all Israel, that they may know that, as I was with Moses, so I will be with thee." The Jordan miracle mirrors the Red Sea crossing (Exodus 14).

adults.question: What instruction was given about the distance to maintain from the ark, and what reason was given?
adults.option_a: About two thousand cubits, that ye may know the way by which ye must go, for ye have not passed this way heretofore
adults.option_b: Stay close to follow its guidance
adults.option_c: One hundred cubits, to show reverence
adults.option_d: Five hundred cubits, to avoid the cloud
adults.explanation: Joshua 3:4 — "Yet there shall be a space between you and it, about two thousand cubits by measure: come not near unto it, that ye may know the way by which ye must go: for ye have not passed this way heretofore."

adults.question: What divine promise was given to Joshua personally at the Jordan crossing?
adults.option_a: God would magnify him in the sight of all Israel as he had magnified Moses
adults.option_b: He would live one hundred and twenty years
adults.option_c: He would never lose a battle
adults.option_d: He would build the first temple
adults.explanation: Joshua 3:7 — "The LORD said unto Joshua, This day will I begin to magnify thee in the sight of all Israel, that they may know that, as I was with Moses, so I will be with thee."

adults.question: What were the twelve men Joshua chose to do, and from where were they selected?
adults.option_a: They were priests to lead worship
adults.option_b: One man from each tribe to take stones from the midst of Jordan
adults.option_c: Twelve warriors to scout the land
adults.option_d: Twelve scribes to record the miracle
adults.explanation: Joshua 4:2 — "Take you twelve men out of the people, out of every tribe a man." (The selection is announced in chapter 3 context.)

adults.question: How does the Jordan crossing theologically connect to Christian baptism in patristic interpretation?
adults.option_a: It has no connection
adults.option_b: Early church fathers saw it as a type of baptism — passing through water into the promised inheritance, as believers pass through baptism into new life
adults.option_c: It represents the law being set aside
adults.option_d: It prefigures the Lord's Supper
adults.explanation: The Jordan crossing (Joshua 3) has been interpreted typologically as prefiguring baptism, just as Paul connects the Red Sea crossing with baptism in 1 Corinthians 10:1-2.

adults.question: Where did the priests bearing the ark stand during the entire crossing?
adults.option_a: They stood firm on dry ground in the midst of Jordan until all the people had passed clean over
adults.option_b: They walked through ahead of the people
adults.option_c: They waited on the east bank
adults.option_d: They moved slowly at the pace of the slowest person
adults.explanation: Joshua 3:17 — "And the priests that bare the ark of the covenant of the LORD stood firm on dry ground in the midst of Jordan, and all the Israelites passed over on dry ground."

adults.question: What did God tell Joshua would happen to the waters once the priests' feet touched dry ground on the other side?
adults.option_a: The waters of Jordan should return unto their place and flow over all his banks as before
adults.option_b: They would freeze
adults.option_c: They would drain into the earth
adults.option_d: They would be parted for forty days
adults.explanation: Joshua 4:18 — "And it came to pass...the feet of the priests were lifted up unto the dry land, that the waters of Jordan returned unto their place, and flowed over all his banks, as they did before."

kids.question: How many stones were taken from the middle of the Jordan River?
kids.explanation: Joshua 4:9 — "And Joshua set up twelve stones in the midst of Jordan, in the place where the feet of the priests which bare the ark of the covenant stood."

kids.question: Where did Joshua set up the twelve stones after crossing the Jordan?
kids.explanation: Joshua 4:20 — "And those twelve stones, which they took out of Jordan, did Joshua pitch in Gilgal."

kids.question: Why were the twelve stones set up?
kids.option_a: To mark the graves of those who died
kids.option_b: As a memorial so future generations would remember God stopping the Jordan
kids.option_c: To build an altar for sacrifices
kids.option_d: To mark the boundary of the promised land
kids.explanation: Joshua 4:7 — "These stones shall be for a memorial unto the children of Israel for ever."

teens.question: What question did Joshua say children would ask when they saw the memorial stones?
teens.option_a: What mean ye by these stones?
teens.explanation: Joshua 4:6 — "That this may be a sign among you, that when your children ask their fathers in time to come, saying, What mean ye by these stones?"

teens.question: How many men bore the ark across the Jordan and when did the waters return?
teens.option_a: The priests bore the ark; the waters returned after their feet touched dry land on the western bank
teens.option_b: Seven priests bore it; waters returned at sunset
teens.option_c: Twelve priests; waters returned after three days
teens.option_d: Levites bore it; waters returned immediately
teens.explanation: Joshua 4:17-18 — "Joshua therefore commanded the priests, saying, Come ye up out of Jordan. And the feet of the priests were lifted up unto the dry land, that the waters of Jordan returned."

teens.question: How many armed men crossed the Jordan, and from which tribes?
teens.option_a: About forty thousand prepared for war, from the tribes of Reuben, Gad, and half of Manasseh
teens.option_c: Twelve thousand from the tribe of Judah
teens.option_d: One hundred thousand from all Israel
teens.explanation: Joshua 4:12-13 — "And the children of Reuben, and the children of Gad, and half the tribe of Manasseh, passed over armed before the children of Israel...about forty thousand prepared for war."

teens.question: What effect did the Jordan crossing have on the kings of the Amorites and Canaanites?
teens.option_a: Their heart melted, neither was there spirit in them any more
teens.explanation: Joshua 5:1 — "All the kings of the Amorites...and all the kings of the Canaanites...heard that the LORD had dried up the waters of Jordan...their heart melted, neither was there spirit in them any more."

teens.question: What day did Israel cross the Jordan, and where did they encamp?
teens.option_a: The first day of the first month, in Gilgal on the east border of Jericho
teens.option_b: The fifteenth day of the seventh month, at Shittim
teens.option_c: The tenth day of the fifth month, at Bethel
teens.option_d: The first day of the third month, at Shechem
teens.explanation: Joshua 4:19 — "And the people came up out of Jordan on the tenth day of the first month, and encamped in Gilgal, in the east border of Jericho."

adults.question: What dual set of memorial stones was placed at the crossing, and where were they located?
adults.option_a: Only one set at Gilgal
adults.option_b: One set in the midst of Jordan where the priests stood, and one set at Gilgal on the western bank
adults.option_c: Stones only on the eastern bank
adults.option_d: Stones only at the tabernacle site
adults.explanation: Joshua 4:9,20 — "Joshua set up twelve stones in the midst of Jordan...And those twelve stones, which they took out of Jordan, did Joshua pitch in Gilgal." Two sets of twelve stones are mentioned.

adults.question: What twofold divine purpose did God state for the Jordan miracle in Joshua 4:24?
adults.option_a: That all the people of the earth might know the hand of the LORD is mighty and that Israel might fear the LORD their God for ever
adults.option_b: To punish Canaan and reward Israel
adults.option_c: To prove Joshua was a prophet and the priests were holy
adults.option_d: To signal the beginning of the Jubilee year
adults.explanation: Joshua 4:24 — "That all the people of the earth might know the hand of the LORD, that it is mighty: that ye might fear the LORD your God for ever."

adults.question: How does the Jordan crossing connect to the stated purpose of magnifying Joshua (Joshua 3:7 and 4:14)?
adults.option_a: The LORD magnified Joshua in the sight of all Israel that day, fulfilling the promise, and they feared him as they feared Moses
adults.option_b: It was a military victory
adults.option_c: It established Joshua as a priest
adults.option_d: It gave Joshua authority to levy taxes
adults.explanation: Joshua 4:14 — "On that day the LORD magnified Joshua in the sight of all Israel; and they feared him, as they feared Moses, all the days of his life."

adults.question: What instruction did God give regarding how fathers were to answer their children about the memorial stones?
adults.option_a: To recount how Israel came over on dry ground and how God dried up Jordan as he had dried the Red Sea
adults.option_b: To say God is great
adults.option_c: To say the stones were built by angels
adults.option_d: To refer the children to the priests
adults.explanation: Joshua 4:22-23 — "Then ye shall let your children know, saying, Israel came over this Jordan on dry land. For the LORD your God dried up the waters of Jordan...as the LORD your God did to the Red sea."

adults.question: What does the specific date — the tenth day of the first month — carry significance with in Israel's calendar?
adults.option_a: It was the day for selecting the Passover lamb (Exodus 12:3), connecting the crossing to redemptive history
adults.option_b: It was the Day of Atonement
adults.option_c: It was the first day of the Feast of Tabernacles
adults.option_d: It was the anniversary of the giving of the law
adults.explanation: Joshua 4:19 states Israel entered Canaan on the tenth of the first month. Exodus 12:3 says the Passover lamb was to be selected on this same day, connecting the entrance into the land with the Passover redemption.

adults.question: How does the two-and-a-half tribes' participation illustrate the principle of corporate obligation in Israel?
adults.option_a: Their participation was optional
adults.option_b: Though their own inheritance was secured east of Jordan, their covenant solidarity required them to serve their brethren until all Israel received rest
adults.option_c: They were being punished for past rebellion
adults.option_d: They participated to earn more land
adults.explanation: Joshua 4:12-13 and Numbers 32 — The Reubenites and Gadites had pledged to Moses to cross armed with their brethren. Their fulfilment illustrates that covenant obligation binds the community together.

adults.question: What rhetorical device do the memorial stones represent in the context of Israel's catechetical tradition?
adults.option_a: They were merely boundary markers
adults.option_b: They were a visible curriculum that prompted children to ask questions, creating structured opportunities to transmit faith and history
adults.option_c: They were monuments to Joshua's glory
adults.option_d: They marked sites for future temples
adults.explanation: Joshua 4:6-7 — "When your children ask their fathers in time to come, saying, What mean ye by these stones? Then ye shall answer them..." The stones function as pedagogical prompts for generational faith transmission.

adults.question: What does the phrase "all the days of his life" in Joshua 4:14 imply about Joshua's continued authority?
adults.option_a: It implies Joshua was immortal
adults.option_b: It establishes that Joshua's God-given authority persisted throughout his entire life, comparable to Moses, grounded in this single miraculous event
adults.option_c: It means Joshua never sinned
adults.option_d: It refers only to his military command
adults.explanation: Joshua 4:14 — "The LORD magnified Joshua in the sight of all Israel...and they feared him, as they feared Moses, all the days of his life." The Jordan crossing established lifelong leadership credibility for Joshua.

kids.question: What special food did God provide for Israel in the desert that stopped when they entered Canaan?
kids.explanation: Joshua 5:12 — "And the manna ceased on the morrow after they had eaten of the old corn of the land...the children of Israel had manna no more."

kids.question: What feast did Israel keep after crossing the Jordan?
kids.explanation: Joshua 5:10 — "And the children of Israel encamped in Gilgal, and kept the passover on the fourteenth day of the month at even in the plains of Jericho."

kids.question: Who appeared to Joshua with a drawn sword before the battle of Jericho?
kids.option_a: The captain of the host of the LORD
kids.explanation: Joshua 5:14 — "And he said, Nay; but as captain of the host of the LORD am I now come."

teens.question: Why did God command the circumcision of Israel at Gilgal?
teens.option_a: Because all the men born in the wilderness had not been circumcised during the forty years of wandering
teens.option_c: Because the Egyptians had stopped the practice
teens.option_d: Because Moses had forbidden it in the desert
teens.explanation: Joshua 5:5-6 — "All the people that were born in the wilderness by the way as they came forth out of Egypt, them they had not circumcised...for the children of Israel walked forty years in the wilderness."

teens.question: What name was given to Gilgal, and what did it mean?
teens.option_a: Gilgal, meaning "I have rolled away the reproach of Egypt"
teens.explanation: Joshua 5:9 — "And the LORD said unto Joshua, This day have I rolled away the reproach of Egypt from off you. Wherefore the name of the place is called Gilgal unto this day."

teens.question: What did Israel eat at their first Passover in Canaan?
teens.option_a: The old corn of the land, unleavened cakes and parched corn
teens.explanation: Joshua 5:11 — "And they did eat of the old corn of the land on the morrow after the passover, unleavened cakes, and parched corn in the selfsame day."

teens.question: What did Joshua do when he recognized the captain of the LORD's host?
teens.option_a: He fell on his face to the earth and worshipped and asked what his lord would say
teens.option_c: He called the elders of Israel
teens.option_d: He ran back to the camp
teens.explanation: Joshua 5:14 — "And Joshua fell on his face to the earth, and did worship, and said unto him, What saith my lord unto his servant?"

teens.question: What command did the captain of the LORD's host give Joshua?
teens.option_a: Loose thy shoe from off thy foot; for the place whereon thou standest is holy
teens.option_c: Fast for seven days before the battle
teens.option_d: Build an altar on this spot
teens.explanation: Joshua 5:15 — "And the captain of the LORD's host said unto Joshua, Loose thy shoe from off thy foot; for the place whereon thou standest is holy. And Joshua did so."

adults.question: What theological significance does the flint knives used at Gilgal have, and what was the location named?
adults.option_a: They symbolised new beginnings
adults.option_b: Flint knives were the traditional instrument; the hill was named Gibeah-haaraloth (hill of the foreskins), marking the restoration of covenant sign
adults.option_c: They were used to mark the boundary stones
adults.option_d: Flint was sacred metal in Israel
adults.explanation: Joshua 5:3 — "And Joshua made him sharp knives, and circumcised the children of Israel at the hill of the foreskins." The use of stone knives (as in Exodus 4:25) connects the rite to ancient covenant tradition.

adults.question: What does "the reproach of Egypt" that God rolled away at Gilgal likely refer to?
adults.option_a: The plagues of Egypt
adults.option_b: Israel's uncircumcised state — making them covenant-deficient and unable to keep Passover — a reproach that Egypt and Canaan could mock
adults.option_c: The memory of slavery
adults.option_d: The shame of Moses' death
adults.explanation: Joshua 5:9 — "This day have I rolled away the reproach of Egypt." The uncircumcised generation could not celebrate Passover (Exodus 12:48), and their covenant deficiency was a reproach now removed upon entering the land.

adults.question: What is the theological significance of manna ceasing on the day after they ate the produce of Canaan?
adults.option_a: It was a coincidence of timing
adults.option_b: It shows God's provision is always precisely calibrated — the supernatural provision ceased exactly when natural provision became available, demonstrating divine faithfulness throughout
adults.option_c: The manna ran out naturally
adults.option_d: It marked the end of the Mosaic era entirely
adults.explanation: Joshua 5:12 — "And the manna ceased on the morrow after they had eaten of the old corn of the land." The precision of timing demonstrates God's intimate providential care.

adults.question: How does the identity of the "captain of the LORD's host" connect to the burning bush episode?
adults.option_a: There is no connection
adults.option_b: Both involve a divine commander on holy ground, with the command to remove shoes linking the two theophanic encounters and suggesting the same divine figure
adults.option_c: Both involve the ark of the covenant
adults.option_d: Both occurred on mountains
adults.explanation: Joshua 5:15 mirrors Exodus 3:5 — "Put off thy shoes from off thy feet, for the place whereon thou standest is holy ground." Many scholars identify this figure as the pre-incarnate Christ or the Angel of the LORD.

adults.question: Why was it strategically risky to circumcise all Israel's fighting men at Gilgal before any battle?
adults.option_a: There was no risk
adults.option_b: Circumcision left all fighting men temporarily incapacitated in enemy territory, as seen with Shechem in Genesis 34, requiring complete faith in God's protection
adults.option_c: The men recovered in one hour
adults.option_d: Only a few men were circumcised at a time
adults.explanation: Joshua 5:8 — "When they had done circumcising all the people...they abode in their places in the camp, till they were whole." The parallel to Genesis 34:25 shows this was a real military vulnerability requiring extraordinary trust.

adults.question: What does the Passover celebration at Gilgal signal about the transition from wilderness to land?
adults.option_a: It was a routine religious observance
adults.option_b: It marks the completion of the Exodus narrative — Israel could now keep the Passover in the promised land as a covenant people fully restored
adults.option_c: It was required before any military action
adults.option_d: It replaced the circumcision rite
adults.explanation: Joshua 5:10 — "And the children of Israel...kept the passover on the fourteenth day of the month at even in the plains of Jericho." This is the third recorded Passover (after Exodus 12 and Numbers 9) and marks Israel's covenantal renewal in the land.

adults.question: What answer did the captain of the LORD's host give when Joshua asked if he was for Israel or for their adversaries?
adults.option_a: He said he was neutral
adults.option_b: He said "Nay" — he came as commander of the LORD's army, transcending human partisan categories
adults.option_c: He said he was sent from God to fight for Israel
adults.option_d: He declared himself for Israel
adults.explanation: Joshua 5:13-14 — "Art thou for us, or for our adversaries? And he said, Nay; but as captain of the host of the LORD am I now come." The answer indicates God is not a tool of human agendas but the sovereign commander.

adults.question: How do the events of Joshua 5 (circumcision, Passover, manna ceasing, theophany) collectively prepare Israel spiritually for the conquest?
adults.option_a: They were administrative preparations only
adults.option_b: They restored covenant identity (circumcision), commemorated redemption (Passover), transitioned from miraculous provision to natural blessing (manna ceased), and established divine authority (theophany) — fully equipping Israel spiritually before any battle
adults.option_c: They were commanded by the priests without divine instruction
adults.option_d: They were simply traditional ceremonies
adults.explanation: Joshua 5 contains four preparatory events — circumcision (v.2-9), Passover (v.10), manna ceasing (v.11-12), and the captain's appearance (v.13-15) — each renewing Israel's covenant standing before the LORD prior to the conquest.

kids.question: How many days did Israel march around Jericho?
kids.explanation: Joshua 6:14-15 — "And the second day they compassed the city once...So they did six days. And it came to pass on the seventh day, that they rose early about the dawning of the day, and compassed the city after the same manner seven times."

kids.question: How many priests carried trumpets before the ark at Jericho?
kids.explanation: Joshua 6:4 — "And seven priests shall bear before the ark seven trumpets of rams' horns."

kids.question: What happened to the walls of Jericho?
kids.option_c: The gates were opened by a traitor
kids.option_d: They were broken by battering rams
kids.explanation: Joshua 6:20 — "The wall fell down flat, so that the people went up into the city, every man straight before him, and they took the city."

teens.question: What were the people told to do on the seventh day of the march?
teens.option_a: Compass the city seven times and then shout when the priests blew the trumpets
teens.option_b: March once around and then attack
teens.option_d: Blow the trumpets continuously for one hour
teens.explanation: Joshua 6:4 — "And ye shall compass the city seven times, and the priests shall blow with the trumpets. And it shall come to pass, that when they make a long blast with the ram's horn...all the people shall shout."

teens.question: What was done with the silver, gold, brass, and iron found in Jericho?
teens.option_a: Consecrated unto the LORD and put into the treasury of the house of the LORD
teens.explanation: Joshua 6:19 — "But all the silver, and gold, and vessels of brass and iron, are consecrated unto the LORD: they shall come into the treasury of the LORD."

teens.question: What specific instruction was given to Joshua about silence during the march?
teens.option_a: The people were to make no noise nor let any word proceed out of their mouth until the day Joshua bid them shout
teens.option_b: To pray quietly throughout the march
teens.option_c: To sing the songs of Moses
teens.explanation: Joshua 6:10 — "And Joshua had commanded the people, saying, Ye shall not shout, nor make any noise with your voice, neither shall any word proceed out of your mouth, until the day I bid you shout; then shall ye shout."

teens.question: Who was saved when Jericho fell?
teens.option_a: All the people of Jericho
teens.option_b: Rahab the harlot and all who were in her house
teens.option_c: The king of Jericho and his household
teens.explanation: Joshua 6:25 — "And Joshua saved Rahab the harlot alive, and her father's household, and all that she had...because she hid the messengers, which Joshua sent to spy out Jericho."

teens.question: What curse did Joshua pronounce over Jericho after its destruction?
teens.option_a: Cursed be the man before the LORD that riseth up and buildeth this city Jericho — he shall lose his sons at its foundations and gates
teens.option_b: No Israelite would ever settle there
teens.option_c: The land would be barren for forty years
teens.option_d: Anyone who entered would be struck blind
teens.explanation: Joshua 6:26 — "Cursed be the man before the LORD, that riseth up and buildeth this city Jericho: he shall lay the foundation thereof in his firstborn, and in his youngest son shall he set up the gates of it."

adults.question: What grammatical and theological feature of Joshua 6:2 signals that Jericho's fall was already certain before any march?
adults.option_a: God used the perfect tense — "I have given into thine hand Jericho" — treating the victory as accomplished fact before it occurred
adults.option_b: God promised to think about it
adults.option_c: God gave a conditional promise
adults.option_d: The text is uncertain
adults.explanation: Joshua 6:2 — "And the LORD said unto Joshua, See, I have given into thine hand Jericho, and the king thereof, and the mighty men of valour." The perfect tense expresses divine certainty of fulfilment.

adults.question: What does the Hebrew word "cherem" (translated "accursed" or "devoted") mean in the context of Jericho?
adults.option_a: A military siege
adults.option_b: Total consecration to God for destruction — the city and its contents were set apart as God's holy war offering, not to be plundered for personal gain
adults.option_c: A peace offering
adults.option_d: A temporary ban on trade
adults.explanation: Joshua 6:17-18 — "And the city shall be accursed (cherem)...lest ye make yourselves accursed, when ye take of the accursed thing." Cherem designated Jericho as the firstfruits of Canaan, entirely devoted to God.

adults.question: How does the fall of Jericho fulfil the theme of holy war (herem) theology in Deuteronomy?
adults.option_a: Deuteronomy has no connection to Joshua
adults.option_b: Deuteronomy 7:1-2 commanded Israel to utterly destroy the Canaanite nations; Jericho as the first city becomes the paradigmatic holy war fulfilment of that command
adults.option_c: It fulfilled the law of the firstfruits only
adults.option_d: It was a purely military campaign with no theological component
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 7:2 — "Thou shalt utterly destroy them." Joshua 6:21 — "They utterly destroyed all that was in the city." Jericho's total destruction establishes the pattern of herem warfare.

adults.question: What prophetic detail in Joshua 6:26 was literally fulfilled centuries later, and where is this recorded?
adults.option_a: Hiel the Bethelite rebuilt Jericho during Ahab's reign, laying the foundation at the cost of his firstborn Abiram and setting up the gates at the cost of Segub, as recorded in 1 Kings 16:34
adults.option_b: It was never fulfilled
adults.option_c: It was fulfilled in the Babylonian captivity
adults.option_d: It was fulfilled in the days of Nehemiah
adults.explanation: 1 Kings 16:34 — "In his days did Hiel the Bethelite build Jericho: he laid the foundation thereof in Abiram his firstborn, and set up the gates thereof in his youngest son Segub, according to the word of the LORD, which he spake by Joshua." This demonstrates the reliability of Joshua's prophecy.

adults.question: How does Rahab's scarlet cord relate to the concept of covenant faithfulness (hesed) in the narrative?
adults.option_a: It was merely a military signal
adults.option_b: The spies swore hesed (lovingkindness) to Rahab contingent on the scarlet cord; God's faithfulness is expressed through Israel honouring its oath to a pagan woman of faith
adults.option_c: It was a priestly symbol
adults.option_d: It replaced the blood of the Passover
adults.explanation: Joshua 2:12 — "Swear unto me by the LORD...that ye will also shew kindness (hesed) unto my father's house." Joshua 6:25 — the oath was kept. God's covenant faithfulness extends even to those outside Israel who trust him.

adults.question: What is the significance of the seven-fold repetition in the Jericho account (seven priests, seven trumpets, seven days, seven circuits on day seven)?
adults.option_a: Seven was simply the Israelite lucky number
adults.option_b: Seven is the biblical number of completeness and divine perfection; the sevenfold pattern signals this victory is entirely God's work, not human military strategy
adults.option_c: It was a random military decision
adults.option_d: It echoed seven days of creation with no further meaning
adults.explanation: The sevenfold pattern in Joshua 6 (7 priests, 7 trumpets, 7 days, 7 circuits) mirrors the 7-day creation pattern and establishes the conquest as a divine creative act — God is creating his holy land.

adults.question: What does Hebrews 11:30 say about the fall of Jericho's walls?
adults.option_a: By faith the walls of Jericho fell down, after they were compassed about seven days
adults.option_b: The walls fell by military strategy
adults.option_c: The walls fell because of an earthquake
adults.option_d: The walls fell because of superior Israelite weapons
adults.explanation: Hebrews 11:30 — "By faith the walls of Jericho fell down, after they were compassed about seven days." The author of Hebrews attributes the miracle entirely to Israel's faith-obedience, not military prowess.

adults.question: What does Rahab's inclusion in the genealogy of Christ (Matthew 1:5) and the hall of faith (Hebrews 11:31) together teach about grace?
adults.option_a: They show social status matters in salvation
adults.option_b: They demonstrate that God's saving grace reaches beyond ethnic and moral barriers — a Canaanite harlot becomes an ancestor of Christ by faith
adults.option_c: They show the law was sufficient for salvation
adults.option_d: They prove that Rahab was secretly an Israelite
adults.explanation: Matthew 1:5 and Hebrews 11:31 together present Rahab — a Gentile and a harlot — as both a woman of faith and a direct ancestor of Jesus Christ, showcasing the breadth of divine grace.

kids.question: What was the name of the man who stole things from Jericho that were supposed to belong to God?
kids.explanation: Joshua 7:1 — "Achan, the son of Carmi, the son of Zabdi, the son of Zerah, of the tribe of Judah, took of the accursed thing."

kids.question: What city did Israel fail to defeat because of Achan's sin?
kids.explanation: Joshua 7:4-5 — "So there went up thither of the people about three thousand men: and they fled before the men of Ai."

kids.question: Where did Achan hide the things he stole?
kids.option_a: In the earth in the midst of his tent
kids.option_b: In a cave outside the camp
kids.option_d: With a neighbour in his tribe
kids.explanation: Joshua 7:21 — "And I took them; and, behold, they are hid in the earth in the midst of my tent."

teens.question: What three items did Achan confess to stealing from Jericho?
teens.option_a: A goodly Babylonish garment, two hundred shekels of silver, and a wedge of gold of fifty shekels
teens.option_d: A golden idol, silver cups, and a sword
teens.explanation: Joshua 7:21 — "When I saw among the spoils a goodly Babylonish garment, and two hundred shekels of silver, and a wedge of gold of fifty shekels weight, then I coveted them, and took them."

teens.question: What did Joshua do before God to express Israel's grief after the defeat at Ai?
teens.option_a: He fell to the earth upon his face before the ark of the LORD and put dust on his head
teens.option_c: He gathered the army to pray
teens.explanation: Joshua 7:6 — "And Joshua rent his clothes, and fell to the earth upon his face before the ark of the LORD until the eventide, he and the elders of Israel, and put dust upon their heads."

teens.question: How was Achan identified as the guilty man?
teens.option_a: A witness came forward
teens.option_b: God revealed him through a process of casting lots by tribe, family, household, and man
teens.option_c: The stolen goods were found in his tent first
teens.explanation: Joshua 7:14-18 — Israel came tribe by tribe, then family, then household, then man by man, and Achan was taken. This systematic narrowing was by divine lot.

teens.question: What was Achan's punishment?
teens.option_a: He was exiled from Israel
teens.option_b: He and his household and all that he had were stoned and burned in the valley of Achor
teens.explanation: Joshua 7:25 — "And Joshua said, Why hast thou troubled us? the LORD shall trouble thee this day. And all Israel stoned him with stones, and burned them with fire, after they had stoned them with stones."

teens.question: What did God tell Joshua was the reason Israel could not stand before their enemies?
teens.option_a: Israel had sinned and taken of the accursed thing and also stolen and dissembled
teens.explanation: Joshua 7:11 — "Israel hath sinned, and they have also transgressed my covenant which I commanded them: for they have even taken of the accursed thing, and have also stolen, and dissembled also."

adults.question: What does Joshua 7:1 reveal about the corporate nature of sin in covenant Israel?
adults.option_a: The anger of the LORD was kindled against the children of Israel — demonstrating that one man's sin brought corporate guilt and consequence upon the whole nation under the covenant
adults.option_b: Only Achan was held responsible
adults.option_c: Sin was always treated individually in Israel
adults.option_d: God was angry at Ai, not Israel
adults.explanation: Joshua 7:1 — "The children of Israel committed a trespass in the accursed thing...and the anger of the LORD was kindled against the children of Israel." The covenant community was corporately implicated by Achan's individual act.

adults.question: What does Achan's confession (Joshua 7:21) reveal about the progression of temptation?
adults.option_a: He was forced to take the items
adults.option_b: He saw, he coveted, and he took — a three-stage sequence mirroring Eve's temptation in Genesis 3:6 and James 1:14-15
adults.option_c: He took the items without noticing their value
adults.option_d: He was tricked by a Canaanite
adults.explanation: Joshua 7:21 — "When I saw...then I coveted them, and took them." This mirrors Genesis 3:6 "she saw...it was to be desired...she took" and James 1:15 "when lust hath conceived, it bringeth forth sin."

adults.question: What theological tension does Joshua's lament in verses 7-9 create?
adults.option_a: It is a purely military concern
adults.option_b: It raises the problem of theodicy and covenant reputation: if Israel is defeated, how will God's name be glorified among the nations?
adults.option_c: Joshua was doubting the existence of God
adults.option_d: No tension — Joshua was sinning by questioning God
adults.explanation: Joshua 7:9 — "What wilt thou do unto thy great name?" Joshua's concern echoes Moses in Exodus 32:12 and Numbers 14:13-16 — God's honour among the nations was at stake, not merely Israel's military success.

adults.question: What does the location "valley of Achor" mean, and how does the prophet Hosea later reinterpret it?
adults.option_a: It means "valley of peace"
adults.option_b: Achor means "trouble"; Hosea 2:15 promises God will make the valley of Achor a door of hope, transforming judgment's site into a symbol of restoration
adults.option_c: It means "valley of fire"
adults.option_d: Achor means "valley of the dead"
adults.explanation: Joshua 7:26 — "called, The valley of Achor, unto this day." Hosea 2:15 — "I will give her...the valley of Achor for a door of hope." The place of Israel's shame becomes a prophetic image of covenant renewal.

adults.question: What does the divine command to sanctify the people (Joshua 7:13) before the lot-casting reveal about approaching God?
adults.option_a: Sanctification was unnecessary
adults.option_b: Corporate guilt requires corporate purification before the holy God can be approached through divination — holiness is prerequisite to divine communication
adults.option_c: It was merely a ritual formality
adults.option_d: Only the priests needed to be sanctified
adults.explanation: Joshua 7:13 — "Sanctify the people, and say, Sanctify yourselves against to morrow: for thus saith the LORD God of Israel, There is an accursed thing in the midst of thee." Holiness must be addressed before Israel could stand in God's presence.

adults.question: How does the destruction of Achan's entire household raise ethical questions about collective punishment?
adults.option_a: The household was innocent and unjustly punished
adults.option_b: The text is debated: some argue the family was complicit since the goods were hidden in the family tent; others see it as a severe covenantal deterrent; Deuteronomy 24:16 later qualifies individual accountability
adults.option_c: Collective punishment is always justified in Scripture
adults.option_d: Only Achan's children were punished
adults.explanation: Joshua 7:24-25 includes Achan's sons and daughters in the punishment. Deuteronomy 24:16 — "The fathers shall not be put to death for the children." Scholars note the family's probable complicity (goods in the family tent) and the unique covenantal context.

adults.question: What name was given to the site of Achan's execution, and what does it signify?
adults.option_a: Valley of Gehenna — place of eternal fire
adults.option_b: The valley of Achor — meaning trouble; it became a perpetual reminder that sin troubles the entire covenant community
adults.option_c: The valley of Rephaim — place of the giants
adults.option_d: The valley of Kidron — place of mourning
adults.explanation: Joshua 7:25-26 — "Why hast thou troubled us? The LORD shall trouble thee this day...Wherefore the name of that place was called, The valley of Achor." Achor (trouble) memorialises the consequence of covenant violation.

adults.question: What does the divine reversal of God's anger after Achan's execution (Joshua 7:26) teach about atonement?
adults.option_a: God's anger was permanent and could not be removed
adults.option_b: Judgment executed according to God's word satisfies divine wrath and restores covenant relationship — the community is cleansed and God's presence can continue with them
adults.option_c: God was pleased merely by the death, not by justice
adults.option_d: God's anger was never real, only symbolic
adults.explanation: Joshua 7:26 — "And the LORD turned from the fierceness of his anger." The execution of covenant justice restored divine favour — a principle pointing toward the satisfaction of divine justice in the atonement of Christ (Romans 3:25-26).

kids.question: How did Israel defeat the city of Ai the second time?
kids.option_a: By using an ambush — hiding soldiers behind the city
kids.option_b: By marching around it seven times
kids.option_d: By bribing the king of Ai
kids.explanation: Joshua 8:2 — "And thou shalt do to Ai and her king as thou didst unto Jericho and her king: only the spoil thereof, and the cattle thereof, shall ye take for a prey unto yourselves: lay thee an ambush for the city behind it."

kids.question: Where did Joshua build an altar to God after defeating Ai?
kids.explanation: Joshua 8:30 — "Then Joshua built an altar unto the LORD God of Israel in mount Ebal."

kids.question: What did Joshua read to all the people after the battle of Ai?
kids.option_a: All the words of the law
kids.explanation: Joshua 8:34 — "And afterward he read all the words of the law, the blessings and cursings, according to all that is written in the book of the law."

teens.question: What was the battle plan God gave Joshua for taking Ai?
teens.option_a: A direct frontal assault at dawn
teens.option_b: Draw the men of Ai out by feigning retreat, while an ambush of thirty thousand men hidden behind the city entered and burned it
teens.option_c: Besiege the city until it surrendered
teens.option_d: Send spies to open the gates from inside
teens.explanation: Joshua 8:4-7 — "And he commanded them, saying, Behold, ye shall lie in wait against the city...when they come out against us, as at the first, then we will flee before them...then ye shall rise up from the ambush, and seize upon the city."

teens.question: How many soldiers did Joshua set in ambush west of Ai?
teens.option_a: Thirty thousand
teens.explanation: Joshua 8:3 — "So Joshua arose, and all the people of war, to go up against Ai: and Joshua chose out thirty thousand mighty men of valour, and sent them away by night."

teens.question: What was the signal Joshua used to direct the ambush to enter Ai?
teens.option_a: He stretched out the javelin in his hand toward Ai
teens.option_c: He sent a runner to the hidden troops
teens.option_d: He lit a signal fire on the hill
teens.explanation: Joshua 8:18 — "And the LORD said unto Joshua, Stretch out the spear that is in thy hand toward Ai; for I will give it into thine hand. And Joshua stretched out the spear that he had in his hand toward the city."

teens.question: What was done with the king of Ai after the battle?
teens.option_a: He was spared and became a servant
teens.option_b: He was hanged on a tree until evening, then his body cast at the entering of the city gate under a heap of stones
teens.option_c: He was sold as a slave in Egypt
teens.option_d: He fled and was never found
teens.explanation: Joshua 8:29 — "And the king of Ai he hanged on a tree until eventide: and as soon as the sun was down, Joshua commanded that they should take his carcase down from the tree...and raise thereon a great heap of stones."

teens.question: What was unique about the altar Joshua built on Mount Ebal according to the law of Moses?
teens.option_a: It was made of cedar wood
teens.option_b: It was built of whole stones upon which no iron tool had been lifted
teens.option_d: It was built on the highest point of the mountain
teens.explanation: Joshua 8:31 — "As Moses the servant of the LORD commanded the children of Israel, as it is written in the book of the law of Moses, an altar of whole stones, over which no man hath lift up any iron: and they offered thereon burnt offerings unto the LORD."

adults.question: What does the specific detail of Joshua holding the javelin extended toward Ai (Joshua 8:18, 26) parallel in the narrative of Moses?
adults.option_a: It has no parallel
adults.option_b: It parallels Moses holding up his staff over the Red Sea and during the battle with Amalek (Exodus 17:11), where the held instrument symbolises divine authority and ongoing victory
adults.option_c: It parallels the ark being raised in the wilderness
adults.option_d: It mirrors Abraham lifting the knife over Isaac
adults.explanation: Joshua 8:26 — "Joshua drew not his hand back...until he had utterly destroyed all the inhabitants of Ai." This mirrors Exodus 17:11 — "when Moses held up his hand, Israel prevailed...when he let down his hand, Amalek prevailed."

adults.question: What permission was granted regarding the spoil of Ai that was not granted at Jericho, and what does this teach?
adults.option_a: No spoil was permitted at Ai
adults.option_b: The cattle and spoil were permitted to be taken for Israel — unlike Jericho's total cherem, showing that Jericho was a unique firstfruits offering while subsequent conquests allowed normal warfare rules
adults.option_c: All spoil at Ai also went to the temple treasury
adults.option_d: Only the priests could take spoil from Ai
adults.explanation: Joshua 8:27 — "Only the cattle and the spoil of that city Israel took for a prey unto themselves, according unto the word of the LORD which he commanded Joshua." Contrast Joshua 6:17-19 where Jericho's spoil was entirely devoted to God.

adults.question: What commandment of Moses was Joshua fulfilling in Joshua 8:30-35, and where was it originally given?
adults.option_a: The law of the Passover
adults.option_b: Deuteronomy 27:2-8 commanded building an altar on Mount Ebal and writing the law on plastered stones when Israel crossed the Jordan
adults.option_c: The law of tithes in Leviticus 27
adults.option_d: The commandment to build the tabernacle
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 27:4-8 — "Therefore it shall be when ye be gone over Jordan...ye shall set up these stones in mount Ebal...and thou shalt write upon the stones all the words of this law." Joshua 8:30-35 faithfully fulfils this command.

adults.question: What was the geographic significance of the covenant renewal at Mounts Ebal and Gerizim?
adults.option_a: They were the highest mountains in Canaan
adults.option_b: They stood in the geographical centre of Canaan, making the public reading of the law a central claiming of the whole land for God's covenant
adults.option_c: They were on the boundary with Philistia
adults.option_d: They were where Moses had died
adults.explanation: Shechem between Ebal and Gerizim lies in the geographic heart of Canaan. Joshua 8:33-35 — the covenant ceremony there claimed the entire land under the law of God, as Deuteronomy 27-28 prescribed.

adults.question: How completely was the law read at the covenant ceremony on Ebal, and who was included?
adults.option_a: Joshua read all that Moses had commanded before all Israel — men, women, children, and the stranger that walked among them — not one word was omitted
adults.option_b: Only the ten commandments were read to the priests
adults.option_c: The law was summarised for the people
adults.option_d: Only the heads of tribes heard the full reading
adults.explanation: Joshua 8:34-35 — "There was not a word of all that Moses commanded, which Joshua read not before all the congregation of Israel, with the women, and the little ones, and the strangers that were conversant among them."

adults.question: What does the inclusion of "strangers" (gerim) in the covenant assembly at Ebal reveal about the scope of Israel's covenant?
adults.option_a: Strangers were excluded from the covenant
adults.option_b: It shows the covenant community was not exclusively ethnic — those who sojourned with Israel were bound by and included in the covenant obligations and blessings
adults.option_c: Strangers heard but were not bound
adults.option_d: Only strangers who converted to Judaism were included
adults.explanation: Joshua 8:35 — "the strangers that were conversant among them." Deuteronomy 31:12 commanded the same: "Gather the people together, men, women, and children, and thy stranger that is within thy gates."

adults.question: What is the typological significance of the altar on Mount Ebal being an altar of uncut stones?
adults.option_a: It was practical — there were no tools available
adults.option_b: Uncut stones represent God's work unaltered by human craft (Exodus 20:25) — the altar must be wholly of God's making, not shaped by human hands, pointing to salvation entirely by God's work
adults.option_c: It was an older pre-Mosaic tradition
adults.option_d: It was to prevent idolatrous symbols being carved
adults.explanation: Exodus 20:25 — "If thou wilt make me an altar of stone, thou shalt not build it of hewn stone: for if thou lift up thy tool upon it, thou hast polluted it." The uncut stone altar embodies the principle that approach to God cannot be improved by human effort.

adults.question: Why is it theologically significant that the covenant ceremony occurs on Mount Ebal — the mount of curses — rather than Mount Gerizim — the mount of blessings?
adults.option_a: It was a mistake by Joshua
adults.option_b: The altar on Ebal acknowledges Israel's sin and need for atonement; burnt and peace offerings on the curse mountain declare that through sacrifice the curse is absorbed, transforming the place of cursing into a site of worship
adults.option_c: Ebal was taller and more visible
adults.option_d: God chose randomly between the two mountains
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 27:13 places the curses on Ebal. Joshua builds the altar there (Joshua 8:30), and burnt offerings and peace offerings are made (8:31). The sacrifices on the mount of curses typologically foreshadow Christ bearing the curse (Galatians 3:13).

kids.question: What group of people tricked Israel into making a peace treaty?
kids.explanation: Joshua 9:3-4 — "And when the inhabitants of Gibeon heard what Joshua had done unto Jericho and to Ai, they did work wilily."

kids.question: What did the Gibeonites pretend to be when they came to Joshua?
kids.option_a: Men who had come from a very far country
kids.option_b: Servants of the king of Ai
kids.explanation: Joshua 9:6 — "They went to Joshua unto the camp at Gilgal, and said unto him, and to the men of Israel, We be come from a far country."

kids.question: What were the Gibeonites made to do as their punishment for the trick?
kids.option_a: Hewers of wood and drawers of water for Israel
kids.explanation: Joshua 9:21 — "Let them live; but let them be hewers of wood and drawers of water unto all the congregation."

teens.question: What false evidence did the Gibeonites bring to appear as if they came from far away?
teens.option_a: Old dry mouldy bread, worn-out sacks and bottles, old shoes, and worn garments
teens.option_c: Spices and goods not found in Canaan
teens.explanation: Joshua 9:12-13 — "This our bread we took hot for our provision out of our houses on the day we came forth to go unto you; but now, behold, it is dry, and it is mouldy: And these bottles of wine...were new; and, behold, they be rent...these our garments and our shoes are become old."

teens.question: What critical mistake did the leaders of Israel make when making the league with the Gibeonites?
teens.option_a: They asked not counsel at the mouth of the LORD
teens.explanation: Joshua 9:14 — "And the men took of their victuals, and asked not counsel at the mouth of the LORD."

teens.question: How soon after making the league did Israel discover the Gibeonites' true identity?
teens.option_a: Three years later
teens.option_b: Three days after, when they came to their cities of Chephirah, Beeroth, and Kirjath-jearim
teens.option_d: When the Gibeonites admitted their deception
teens.explanation: Joshua 9:16-17 — "It came to pass at the end of three days after they had made a league with them, that they heard that they were their neighbours, and that they dwelt among them."

teens.question: Why could not the Israelites attack the Gibeonites once the deception was discovered?
teens.option_a: The princes had sworn an oath by the LORD God of Israel and could not break it
teens.explanation: Joshua 9:19 — "All the princes said unto all the congregation, We have sworn unto them by the LORD God of Israel: now therefore we may not touch them."

teens.question: What specific task of service were the Gibeonites assigned within God's sanctuary?
teens.option_a: Hewers of wood and drawers of water for the house of God and congregation
teens.explanation: Joshua 9:23,27 — "Now therefore ye are cursed, and there shall none of you be freed from being bondmen, and hewers of wood and drawers of water for the house of my God...hewers of wood and drawers of water for the congregation, and for the altar of the LORD."

adults.question: What does Joshua 9:14 reveal as the hermeneutical lesson of the Gibeonite episode?
adults.option_a: That Israelites were gullible
adults.option_b: That seeking God's counsel before major decisions is essential — neglecting prayer opens the door to being deceived by appearances
adults.option_c: That Gibeon was a great nation
adults.option_d: That Joshua was at fault for everything
adults.explanation: Joshua 9:14 — "The men took of their victuals, and asked not counsel at the mouth of the LORD." The text explicitly identifies this as the cause of the error, teaching that human judgment without divine guidance is insufficient.

adults.question: What was the Gibeonites' stated motivation for the deception, and what does it reveal about their faith?
adults.option_a: They wanted to exploit Israel
adults.option_b: They had heard of the LORD's mighty deeds and feared for their lives — Rahab-like faith led them to seek refuge under the God of Israel
adults.option_c: They were purely self-serving
adults.option_d: They wanted to be slaves
adults.explanation: Joshua 9:24 — "It was certainly told thy servants, how that the LORD thy God commanded his servant Moses to give you all the land, and to destroy all the inhabitants of the land before you: therefore we were sore afraid of our lives because of you."

adults.question: What covenant principle was tested by the Gibeonite treaty, and how did God later vindicate it?
adults.option_a: The treaty had no lasting significance
adults.option_b: The inviolability of oaths sworn in God's name — God later punished Saul's house for violating it (2 Samuel 21:1-2), showing that sworn oaths bind even when obtained by fraud
adults.option_c: God permitted breaking oaths made under deception
adults.option_d: The Gibeonites eventually forfeited their protection
adults.explanation: 2 Samuel 21:1-2 — "There is blood upon Saul, and upon his house, because he slew the Gibeonites." Centuries later, God sent famine on Israel for Saul breaking the Joshua 9 oath, confirming its binding nature even across generations.

adults.question: How does the Gibeonite deception relate to the Deuteronomic command to destroy the Canaanite nations?
adults.option_a: It shows Deuteronomy was wrong
adults.option_b: Deuteronomy 20:10-18 permitted peace treaties with distant cities but commanded destruction of the seven Canaanite nations — the Gibeonites exploited this distinction by claiming to be from afar
adults.option_c: The Gibeonites were not Canaanites
adults.option_d: The treaty automatically fulfilled the destruction command
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 20:10-11 permits making peace with distant cities. The Gibeonites (Joshua 9:7 identifies them as Hivites, a Canaanite group) exploited this legal distinction, knowing a distant-city claim would secure a treaty.

adults.question: What does Joshua's response to the Gibeonites after discovering the deception reveal about his commitment to covenant faithfulness?
adults.option_a: He immediately broke the treaty
adults.option_b: He maintained the oath despite the deception, showing that covenant integrity requires honouring sworn commitments even when wrongfully obtained, because the oath was sworn by God's name
adults.option_c: He sought a legal way to void the treaty
adults.option_d: He punished the Gibeonites severely and then annulled the treaty
adults.explanation: Joshua 9:19-20 — "We have sworn unto them by the LORD God of Israel: now therefore we may not touch them...lest wrath be upon us, because of the oath which we sware unto them." Covenant faithfulness superseded the offence of deception.

adults.question: What is the irony of the Gibeonites' assigned role as "hewers of wood and drawers of water for the house of my God"?
adults.option_a: It was not ironic — it was purely punitive
adults.option_b: Those who came seeking to serve as servants ended up serving the very temple of the God they feared — their deception led them into proximity with the God of Israel, potentially becoming a means of their salvation
adults.option_c: The Gibeonites were angered by this assignment
adults.option_d: The role was temporary only
adults.explanation: Joshua 9:27 — "hewers of wood and drawers of water for the congregation, and for the altar of the LORD." The Gibeonites' fear of God led them into covenantal proximity with Israel's worship — a remarkable grace hidden within judgment.

adults.question: What does the reaction of the congregation against the princes (Joshua 9:18) reveal about Israel's corporate ethic?
adults.option_a: The people were right to be angry
adults.option_b: It shows tension between popular outrage and leadership accountability to sworn oaths — the princes rightly overrode popular opinion because covenant faithfulness to God's name took precedence
adults.option_c: The princes should have annulled the treaty
adults.option_d: The congregation had the final authority
adults.explanation: Joshua 9:18 — "The whole congregation murmured against the princes." Numbers 30:2 — "If a man vow a vow unto the LORD...he shall not break his word." The princes stood firm against popular pressure because the oath was sworn by God's name.

adults.question: How does the Gibeonite story prefigure the place of Gentiles in redemptive history?
adults.option_a: It has no redemptive significance
adults.option_b: Gibeonites — a condemned Canaanite people — found grace through faith-driven submission to Israel's God; their descendants served at the temple, foreshadowing Gentiles grafted into the covenant community by faith
adults.option_c: It shows Gentiles could never enter the covenant
adults.option_d: It was a unique exception with no broader meaning
adults.explanation: Joshua 9:24-27 — the Gibeonites' fearful faith brought them into the service of the LORD's altar. Isaiah 56:6-7 later promises Gentiles who join themselves to the LORD will minister in his temple, fulfilling a pattern begun at Gibeon.

kids.question: What miracle did God perform to help Joshua fight the five kings?
kids.option_a: He made the sun and moon stand still
kids.explanation: Joshua 10:13 — "And the sun stood still, and the moon stayed, until the people had avenged themselves upon their enemies."

kids.question: Who were the five kings that joined together to fight against Gibeon?
kids.option_a: Kings of Jerusalem, Hebron, Jarmuth, Lachish, and Eglon
kids.option_b: Kings of Egypt, Babylon, Assyria, Persia, and Media
kids.option_c: Kings of Jericho, Ai, Bethel, Gibeon, and Lachish
kids.option_d: Kings of Canaan, Hittites, Amorites, Perizzites, and Hivites
kids.explanation: Joshua 10:3-5 — "Wherefore Adoni-zedek king of Jerusalem...Hoham king of Hebron...Piram king of Jarmuth...Japhia king of Lachish...Debir king of Eglon, gathered themselves together...and fought against Gibeon."

kids.question: Where were the five kings hiding when Israel found them?
kids.explanation: Joshua 10:16 — "But these five kings fled, and hid themselves in a cave at Makkedah."

teens.question: Why did the five Canaanite kings attack Gibeon?
teens.option_a: Because Gibeon had made peace with Israel and they feared other cities would do the same
teens.option_c: Because the Gibeonites had insulted their king
teens.option_d: Because Gibeon controlled the water supply
teens.explanation: Joshua 10:1-2 — "Adoni-zedek king of Jerusalem had heard how Joshua had taken Ai...and how the inhabitants of Gibeon had made peace with Israel...wherefore the five kings of the Amorites...gathered themselves together."

teens.question: What did the LORD do to the enemies of Israel with great stones from heaven?
teens.option_a: He cast down great stones of hail upon them, so that more died from hailstones than from Israel's sword
teens.option_c: He caused an earthquake under them
teens.explanation: Joshua 10:11 — "The LORD cast down great stones from heaven upon them unto Azekah, and they died: they were more which died with hailstones than they whom the children of Israel slew with the sword."

teens.question: What did Joshua command the sun and moon to do, and how long did the sun stop?
teens.option_a: Sun to stand still upon Gibeon and moon over Ajalon; the sun hastened not to go down about a whole day
teens.option_b: To brighten and the sun stopped for one hour
teens.option_c: To set quickly so Israel could rest
teens.option_d: To rise twice on the same day
teens.explanation: Joshua 10:12-13 — "Sun, stand thou still upon Gibeon; and thou, Moon, in the valley of Ajalon...So the sun stood still in the midst of heaven, and hasted not to go down about a whole day."

teens.question: What was done with the five kings at Makkedah?
teens.option_a: Joshua smote them, slew them, hanged them on five trees until evening, then cast them into the cave and rolled great stones on the mouth
teens.option_c: They were spared and made servants
teens.explanation: Joshua 10:26-27 — "Joshua smote them, and slew them, and hanged them on five trees...Joshua commanded, and they took them down off the trees, and cast them into the cave wherein they had been hid, and laid great stones in the cave's mouth."

teens.question: What cities did Joshua conquer in the southern campaign of chapter 10?
teens.option_a: Makkedah, Libnah, Lachish, Eglon, Hebron, and Debir
teens.explanation: Joshua 10:28-39 — "And that day Joshua took Makkedah...then Joshua passed from Makkedah, and all Israel with him, unto Libnah...and from Libnah unto Lachish...and from Lachish unto Eglon...and from Eglon unto Hebron...and Joshua returned, and all Israel with him, to Debir."

adults.question: What does Joshua 10:14 declare is the unique aspect of the long day miracle?
adults.option_a: There was no day like that before it or after it that the LORD hearkened unto the voice of a man — the LORD fought for Israel
adults.option_b: That it was the first miracle in Canaan
adults.option_c: That it lasted exactly twelve hours
adults.option_d: That the moon also stood still for the first time
adults.explanation: Joshua 10:14 — "And there was no day like that before it or after it, that the LORD hearkened unto the voice of a man: for the LORD fought for Israel."

adults.question: What does the phrase "the LORD hearkened unto the voice of a man" (Joshua 10:14) reveal about prayer and divine responsiveness?
adults.option_a: God never hears human requests
adults.option_b: God, who governs all creation, sovereignly responded to Joshua's bold intercessory command, showing that earnest prayer can shape the course of cosmic events according to God's will
adults.option_c: Joshua had special magical powers
adults.option_d: This was a coincidence of natural events
adults.explanation: Joshua 10:12-14 — Joshua spoke to the LORD (v.12) and the LORD hearkened (v.14). James 5:16 — "The effectual fervent prayer of a righteous man availeth much." God's sovereignty does not eliminate but enables effective prayer.

adults.question: What ancient source does Joshua 10:13 cite as corroborating the long-day account, and what does this citation suggest?
adults.option_a: The priestly records of the tabernacle
